question: What visual evidence on the blackboard confirms the course number and URL, and how does the professor connect this to the course's focus on large-scale inputs like the human genome?
answer: The blackboard clearly shows '6.006' and the URL 'http://courses.csail.mit.edu/6.006', confirming the course identity. The professor explains that the course focuses on efficient procedures for solving problems on large inputs, giving the human genome — which has a billion letters in its alphabet — as a concrete example of what 'large' means in the 21st century context.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard frame showing course number 6.006 and URL
Professor describes large inputs including the human genome with a billion letters

question: The blackboard shows 'Classic data structures & classical algorithms' as a course topic. What specific data structures does the professor name in the lecture, and how does he describe their longevity?
answer: The blackboard lists classic data structures as a course topic. The professor specifically names binary search trees, hash tables (called dictionaries in Python), and balanced binary search trees. He describes these as data structures invented many decades ago that have stood the test of time and continue to be useful, noting that the course will also augment them in various ways to make them more efficient for certain kinds of problems.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard frame listing 'Classic data structures & classical algorithms' as course content
Professor names binary search trees, hash tables, balanced binary search trees and describes their longevity

question: The blackboard displays 'Shortest paths: Caltech - MIT' as a module topic. What alternative application does the professor suggest for the shortest paths problem set in the current term, and what algorithmic technique is listed alongside it?
answer: The blackboard shows 'Shortest paths: Caltech - MIT' as a module. The professor suggests that instead of finding the shortest path from Caltech to MIT as in previous terms, they might give students a map of Boston and have them figure out if Paul Revere used a shortest path to get to his destination. Dynamic programming is listed alongside shortest paths as another module, described as an important algorithm design technique used in many problems including image compression.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard showing 'Shortest paths: Caltech - MIT' and 'Dyn programming: Image compression'
Professor describes Paul Revere alternative and dynamic programming for image compression

question: The blackboard shows the recurrence relation T(n) = T(n/2) + Θ(1) with base case T(1) = Θ(1). What runtime does the professor derive from this, and how does he demonstrate the practical difference from the Θ(n) algorithm?
answer: From the recurrence T(n) = T(n/2) + Θ(1) with base case T(1) = Θ(1), the professor derives that expanding the recurrence log base 2 of n times gives a total complexity of Θ(log₂n). He demonstrates the practical difference by stating that when run on n being 10 million elements, the Θ(n) straightforward algorithm takes 13 seconds, while the Θ(log n) divide-and-conquer algorithm takes only 0.001 seconds — a huge difference that he says is essentially the difference between 2 raised to n and n.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard showing T(n)=T(n/2)+Θ(1), base case T(1)=Θ(1), and Θ(log₂n) result
Professor states 13 seconds vs 0.001 seconds for 10 million elements

question: The blackboard frame for the 2D version shows a grid with labels for rows and columns and defines a 2D peak condition. What does the professor say is the worst-case complexity of the greedy ascent algorithm for this problem, and what does it reduce to when the matrix dimensions are equal?
answer: The blackboard shows the 2D version with an n-row by m-column matrix and defines a 2D peak using conditions a ≥ b, a ≥ c, a ≥ d, a ≥ e. The professor states that a greedy ascent algorithm for the 2D peak-finding problem has Θ(nm) complexity in the worst case, because one may end up touching a large fraction of all elements in the matrix. When N equals M, this reduces to Θ(n²) complexity, which is significantly worse.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard showing 2D version grid with n rows and m columns and 2D peak definition
Professor states Θ(nm) and Θ(n²) when m=n for greedy ascent algorithm

question: The blackboard shows 'Attempt #2' with the steps for the correct 2D divide-and-conquer algorithm. Why does the professor say this algorithm is correct when the first attempt (finding a 1D peak on row i) was not, using the specific numerical example visible on the board?
answer: The blackboard for Attempt #2 shows steps: pick middle column j = m/2, find global max on column j at (i,j), then compare (i,j-1), (i,j), (i,j+1). The professor explains this is correct because finding the global maximum in the column means the element (i,j) has already been compared to all adjacent elements above and below. Then checking left and right neighbors completes all four comparisons needed for a 2D peak. The first attempt failed because, as shown with the example matrix containing values like 14, 15, 19, a 1D peak on a row (such as 14) need not be a 2D peak since elements in adjacent columns (like 19) can be larger.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard showing Attempt #2 steps with global max on column and comparison of neighbors
Professor explains why first attempt fails using 14 not being a 2D peak example

question: The blackboard lists 'Hashing' and 'Genome comparison' as course modules. What specific biological example does the professor use to illustrate why efficiency is essential for hashing-based genome comparison, and what percentage similarity does he cite?
answer: The blackboard lists hashing and genome comparison as course modules. The professor uses the comparison of the human genome to the rat genome as a specific biological example, citing that they are approximately 99% similar — which he describes as 'kind of amazing.' He emphasizes that because these genomes are so large, efficiency in the comparison methods is absolutely essential: if the complexity is not kept low enough, the program simply won't be able to finish running by the time the problem set is due.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard frame listing Hashing and Genome comparison as course modules
Professor describes human genome vs rat genome comparison being 99% similar and need for efficiency

question: What text appears on the blackboard when the professor first introduces the specific problem that will be the focus of the lecture?
answer: When the professor introduces the main problem for the lecture, the blackboard displays the text 'Peak finder' written in white chalk, with 'Peak' in a larger font size than 'finder.' The board is otherwise blank at that moment, drawing attention specifically to the problem name and signaling the transition from course overview to the technical content of the lecture.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard frame showing 'Peak finder' written in white chalk with Peak in larger font

question: What specific equation and base case are written on the blackboard when the professor derives the complexity of the correct 2D divide-and-conquer peak-finding algorithm?
answer: The blackboard shows the recurrence relation T(n, m) = T(n, m/2) + Θ(n), with the base case T(n, 1) = Θ(n). A third line then shows the solution T(n, m) = Θ(n log₂ m), indicating that the overall complexity of the correct 2D divide-and-conquer algorithm is Θ(n log₂ m), which is derived by recognizing that Θ(n) work is added log₂ m times as the number of columns is repeatedly halved.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard showing T(n,m)=T(n,m/2)+Θ(n), base case T(n,1)=Θ(n), and solution Θ(n log₂ m)

question: How does the professor define the 21st-century notion of 'large' input size at the beginning of the lecture, and how does he contrast it with what was considered large in previous decades?
answer: The professor states that the 21st-century definition of 'large' is roughly a trillion. He contrasts this by humorously noting that when he was a student, 'large' meant about a thousand, and when Professor Erik Demaine was a student, 'large' meant about a million. He further speculates that by the time current students are professors teaching the class, a trillion will seem small and they will be concerned with inputs on the order of 10 to the 18.

ground_truth_spans.description: Professor says 21st century definition of large is a trillion and contrasts with thousand and million
Professor speculates future students will deal with 10^18 as common input size

question: What is the straightforward algorithm's worst-case complexity for 1D peak finding, and what reasoning does the professor give for why this complexity is unavoidable in the worst case?
answer: The straightforward algorithm for 1D peak finding has worst-case complexity of Θ(n). The professor explains this is unavoidable in the worst case because the algorithm starts from the left and walks across the array, and the peak may be all the way on the right end. In that case, the algorithm must look at all n elements, scanning positions one through n from left to right before finding the peak. If the peak is in the middle, the algorithm looks at approximately n/2 elements, but Θ(n) captures the true worst case where the peak is at the rightmost position.

ground_truth_spans.description: Professor explains worst case is when peak is all the way on the right requiring n element scans
Professor states Θ(n) worst-case complexity for straightforward algorithm and defines theta vs Big O

question: Why does the professor argue that the first divide-and-conquer attempt for 2D peak finding is incorrect, and what specific counterexample does he use to demonstrate the failure?
answer: The professor argues the first attempt is incorrect because a 2D peak may not exist on the row selected by the algorithm. His specific counterexample uses a matrix where, starting from a chosen middle row, the algorithm finds element 12 as a 1D peak (because 10 is above it and 11 flanks it, making 12 locally maximal in that column), but 19 is a larger element elsewhere in the matrix. Then when picking row i and finding a 1D peak on that row, the algorithm returns 14, but 14 is not a 2D peak because other adjacent elements in neighboring columns exceed it. The algorithm is described as efficient but incorrect — and the professor emphasizes that being correct and inefficient is better than being incorrect and efficient.

ground_truth_spans.description: Professor explains 2D peak may not exist on row i as the core problem with attempt 1
Professor gives counterexample: 14 returned as peak on row i but 14 is not a 2D peak

question: What does the professor say about the programming language used for problem sets in 6.006, and how does he describe the structure of each problem set in terms of its components?
answer: The professor states that problem sets in 6.006 involve real implementations in Python. Each problem set has both a theory part and a programming part to them, so the two components are meant to tie together. The kinds of things discussed in lectures and recitations are directly connected to the theory parts of the problem sets, and students will be programming the algorithms discussed in lecture, augmenting them, running them, and figuring out whether they work well on large inputs.

ground_truth_spans.description: Professor describes Python implementations and theory plus programming parts of problem sets

question: What specific RSA-related example does the professor give when introducing the numerics module, and what connection does he make to a widely used internet protocol?
answer: The professor explains that RSA encryption is used in SSL, and specifically when users access HTTPS websites, RSA is running at the back end. In RSA, prime numbers with thousands of bits in their representation are used, which means 32 bits or even 64 bits are insufficient. This motivates the numerics module, which covers how to handle so-called 'infinite precision numbers' — numbers with thousands of bits — and how to write algorithms in Python that can deal with such large number representations.

ground_truth_spans.description: Professor describes RSA encryption used in SSL/HTTPS with prime numbers thousands of bits long
